summary: The post argues that new engineering managers often focus too heavily on solving immediate problems while neglecting the crucial leadership skill of articulating a vision for their team's future. Through a conversation with a tech lead, Fournier identifies that technical managers rarely spend time thinking about or communicating where their team should be headed beyond the product roadmap. She distinguishes between management (handling today's reality) and leadership (providing hope through vision), noting that even excellent managers cannot inspire true team loyalty without the latter. The solution is simple but not easy: deliberately practice thinking about and articulating the future.
key_points: Moving from individual contributor to manager is a career change, not just a promotion, and requires developing new skills beyond solving technical problems
Great management skills alone cannot inspire team loyalty and passion—leadership and vision are essential additions
Technical managers are comfortable with reality but often avoid articulating future vision because it feels risky and uncertain
Leadership can be learned through deliberate practice: stepping away from daily work to think about and articulate a future vision
The most important thing a manager can do is regularly communicate not just what the team is doing, but where it's going
money_quotes: You can be the greatest manager in the world, but without leadership and vision, your team will not be truly sticky to you.
Too often first-time tech managers focus on reality. We're comfortable with reality, reality is our bread and butter.
When you focus on nothing but today's problems, even if you are a great manager who does everything right, you are unlikely to motivate your team to greatness.
Reacting to today is so easy, and trying to predict the future seems really hard.
Fortunately, leadership is not a skill you have to be born with. It just requires that you identify the future and articulate it.
Get away from your keyboard. Force yourself to sit in an empty room with a whiteboard or a pen and paper and write some ideas down.
key_insight: The transition from technical contributor to effective leader requires moving beyond solving today's problems to deliberately practicing the articulation of a compelling future vision for your team.
